summary: Joe Reis reflects on the rise and fall of the Modern Data Stack, from its promising beginnings with Redshift in 2012 through the ZIRP-fueled explosion of undifferentiated startups, to its current status as an obsolete term. While acknowledging the MDS ultimately succeeded by normalizing cloud-native analytics, he criticizes how the era promoted poor data practices and tool sprawl over fundamentals like data modeling.
key_points: Redshift's 2012 launch marked the true beginning of the MDS by making data warehousing accessible and affordable
The COVID-era zero interest rate policy led to a flood of undifferentiated MDS startups competing in tiny verticals
Best practices regressed as new practitioners prioritized tool usage over fundamentals like data modeling
The MDS ultimately won by normalizing modular, cloud-based analytics - making the term itself obsolete
Data teams are now being cut and expensive tool sprawl gutted as the industry returns to fundamentals
money_quotes: If you disagree, please travel with me in a time machine to 2011, and let's negotiate an on-prem data warehouse and ETL tooling contract with a giant tech vendor of that era.
The MDS made it much easier to work with data, and it also made it much easier to do dumb things more quickly.
I feel like the MDS made it easy for the data industry to go backward, allowing data teams to have a ton of convenient tooling while adding questionable value to their organization.

Data is a thinking person's game. We're finally returning to the discipline and rigor we had in the good old days when we were thoughtful about data instead of just throwing more compute and technology at the problem.
The thing is, the MDS won. It normalized what it's supposed to do - modular, easy-to-use, cloud-based analytical tooling.
key_insight: The Modern Data Stack succeeded so thoroughly at normalizing cloud-native analytics that the term itself became obsolete, but left behind a generation of practitioners who prioritized tooling over data fundamentals.
